questions.prompt: A client onboarding a new CRM. The CRM says 'add `include:_spf.crm-vendor.example` to your SPF.' The existing zone has `"v=spf1 include:spf.protection.outlook.com -all"`. What's the right way to add the CRM's include?
choices: Add a second TXT record with `"v=spf1 include:_spf.crm-vendor.example -all"`.
Edit the existing TXT to add the new include: `"v=spf1 include:spf.protection.outlook.com include:_spf.crm-vendor.example -all"`. Verify the lookup count is under 10.
Delete the old SPF and add a new one with both includes.
questions.explanation: Two SPF records make SPF evaluate as permerror — no protection at all. Multiple senders go in the single record's include list. `+all` authorises everyone (never right); delete-then-add leaves a window with no SPF.

questions.prompt: You count the DNS lookups in a published SPF record and reach 11. What happens at receivers?
choices: SPF still evaluates; the limit is advisory.
SPF evaluates as `permerror`. Receivers treat the domain as if SPF protection isn't in place.
The first 10 lookups count; the 11th is ignored.
It only matters for receivers that enforce DMARC `p=reject`.
questions.explanation: The 10-lookup limit is enforced. Over-limit SPF has no protective value even though the record is published. permerror affects SPF evaluation regardless of DMARC enforcement mode.

questions.prompt: Which SPF policy term should you *never* use as a 'catch-all' for legitimate senders?
questions.explanation: +all authorises everyone, including attackers. It defeats SPF entirely. The right policy terms are -all (strict) or ~all (soft fail); ?all is neutral and provides no protection but at least doesn't authorise attackers.
